title: Mistral Small 3.2
oneLiner: Apache-licensed small open model for practical instruction following, local inference, and agent experiments.
summary: Mistral Small 3.2 is a compact open model release from Mistral AI, useful for teams that want a practical model candidate for local inference, instruction-following tests, and cost-sensitive AI workflows.
whyItMatters: Mistral Small 3.2 matters because small open models often decide whether an AI feature can be cheap, fast, and self-hosted. It gives builders a model to evaluate before jumping to larger and more expensive inference stacks.
bestFor: Developers comparing compact open models for local or self-hosted inference
Teams that need a lower-cost model for tool workflows and assistant prototypes
Builders who want Mistral ecosystem compatibility with permissive licensing
notFor: Teams that need the strongest frontier reasoning model available
Users who want a finished consumer assistant rather than a model to integrate
seoTitle: Mistral Small 3.2: Compact open model for local AI
seoDescription: Mistral Small 3.2 profile: what it is, when to use it, official links, command line, and structured OpenAgent data for open AI builders.
shareDescription: Compact Apache-licensed open model for local inference and agent experiments.

coreStrengths.title: Compact model for practical evaluation
coreStrengths.description: Mistral Small 3.2 is positioned as a smaller model that can fit more cost-sensitive inference plans.
coreStrengths.whyItMatters: Many AI products need reliable enough models, not the largest possible model.

coreStrengths.title: Permissive open model path
coreStrengths.description: The release is associated with Apache-2.0 licensing and Mistral's open model ecosystem.
coreStrengths.whyItMatters: License clarity is one of the first filters for teams evaluating open model adoption.

coreStrengths.title: Good candidate for agent and assistant prototypes
coreStrengths.description: Compact instruction-following models are useful for early tool workflows, routing, and assistant behavior tests.
coreStrengths.whyItMatters: A small model can reduce iteration cost while a team is still proving the workflow.

useCaseNotes.title: Local assistant experiments
useCaseNotes.description: Use it to test whether a smaller open model is enough for a product feature before paying for larger inference.

useCaseNotes.title: Routing and lightweight agent tasks
useCaseNotes.description: Evaluate it for classification, summarization, tool selection, and other tasks that do not always require a frontier model.

useCaseNotes.title: Self-hosted inference planning
useCaseNotes.description: Use the model as part of a benchmark set when deciding what can run on your own infrastructure.

compareNotes.title: Choose Mistral Small 3.2 when size and cost matter
compareNotes.summary: Larger models may win on difficult reasoning, but Mistral Small 3.2 is worth testing when local cost, latency, and integration simplicity are more important.

gettingStarted.label: Open the Hugging Face model page

commandLine.label: Install Mistral inference tooling
commandLine.description: Use the official inference package as a starting point, then follow the model-specific instructions from the official model card.

seoArticle.intro: Mistral Small 3.2 is worth tracking because small open models are often the difference between a prototype that is interesting and a product feature that is affordable. It is a candidate for teams evaluating local or self-hosted inference without starting from the largest model available.
seoArticle.whatItIs: Mistral Small 3.2 is a compact open model release from Mistral AI. It is useful as a practical instruction-following model candidate for local inference, self-hosting tests, and early agent workflows.
seoArticle.whyItMatters: Open model selection is not only about top benchmark scores. Many teams need a model that is small enough to run cheaply, permissive enough to adopt, and capable enough for real workflow tasks. Mistral Small 3.2 fits that evaluation slot.
seoArticle.howItWorks: Start with the official model card, then run a small benchmark on your own prompts. Test latency, tool-output reliability, summarization quality, and failure modes before deciding whether it should handle production traffic.

useCases.title: Cost-sensitive assistant features
useCases.description: Use it when the workload does not justify a larger model on every request.

useCases.title: Local model comparison
useCases.description: Benchmark it against Gemma, Qwen, DeepSeek, and OLMo variants on your own hardware.

useCases.title: Agent routing and support tasks
useCases.description: Test it for smaller agent subtasks such as routing, summarizing context, and creating structured outputs.

alternatives.title: Use larger models for harder reasoning
alternatives.summary: Mistral Small 3.2 is not automatically the strongest reasoning model; it is strongest when the deployment tradeoff favors compactness and cost.

faq.question: Is Mistral Small 3.2 open source?
faq.answer: The public model card should be treated as the source of truth for license terms. This OpenAgent profile tracks it as an Apache-licensed open model candidate.

faq.question: Why use a smaller model?
faq.answer: Smaller models can be cheaper, faster, and easier to self-host, especially for workflows that do not need frontier reasoning on every request.

faq.question: Should I use Mistral Small 3.2 for agents?
faq.answer: It is worth testing for lightweight agent subtasks, but teams should benchmark tool use and structured output behavior before relying on it.
